title: Optimizing a Mixed Workload's Cost
description:
## Baseline Plus Bursts

A company runs a web platform on EC2 with two distinct demand profiles:

- A **steady 24/7 baseline** of roughly 20 instances that has been stable for over a year and is expected to continue.
- A **spiky, batch image-processing tier** that runs irregularly, can be safely interrupted and restarted, and tolerates instances disappearing mid-run.

The finance team wants the lowest sustainable cost without harming the user-facing service. Which **purchasing strategy** best fits each tier?

options.text: Cover the 24/7 baseline with a **Compute Savings Plan (or Reserved Instances)** for the deep discount on predictable usage, and run the interruptible batch tier on **Spot Instances**.
Run everything On-Demand to keep maximum flexibility, then ask AWS for a retroactive discount at the end of the year.
Put the steady baseline on **Spot Instances** to get the biggest discount, and run the interruptible batch jobs on **Reserved Instances** for guaranteed capacity.
Use **Dedicated Hosts** for both tiers so that all workloads run on single-tenant hardware, which is always the cheapest option.

explanation: Match each demand profile to the right pricing model. A **predictable, always-on baseline** is the textbook case for a **Savings Plan or Reserved Instances**: you commit to a steady level of usage for 1 or 3 years and receive a large discount over On-Demand. An **interruption-tolerant** batch workload is the textbook case for **Spot Instances**, which offer up to ~90% off On-Demand in exchange for AWS being able to reclaim capacity with two minutes' notice. Option B leaves the steady baseline at full On-Demand price (no retroactive discounts exist). Option C inverts the strategy: putting your always-on, user-facing baseline on Spot risks interruptions to production, and committing to RIs for irregular batch capacity wastes the commitment when it's idle. Option D is wrong because **Dedicated Hosts** are a *more* expensive, single-tenant option used for licensing/compliance, not a cost-minimization default.

hints.title: Predictable vs. interruptible
hints.body: Commitment-based pricing (Savings Plans/RIs) rewards predictable, steady usage. Spot rewards workloads that don't mind being interrupted.

hints.title: Never put production on Spot if it can't be interrupted
hints.body: Spot capacity can be reclaimed with ~2 minutes' notice. That's fine for retryable batch jobs, dangerous for a steady user-facing baseline.
